aMaths 2 :: Linear Algebra
1. Parallelogram Law of Vector Addition 2. Matrix Multiplication 3. Properties of Determinant 4. Matrix Representation of a System of Linear Equations 5. Solution for a System of Linear Equations 6. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Cramer's Rule 7. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Using Invertible Coefficient Matrix 8. Elementary Row and Column Operations 9. Row Echelon Form 10. Reduced Row Echelon Form 11. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Gaussian Elimination Method 12. Vector Space 13. Properties of Vector Space 14. Subspace of a Vector Space 15. Linear Combination 16. Linear independence and Linear dependence. 17. Span Set 18. Basis and Dimension of a Vector Space 19. Finding a Basis Set 20. Rank of a Matrix 21. Nullity of a Matrix 22. Rank-Nullity Theorem of Matrices 23. Dot Product 24. Length of a Vector 25. Euclilean Distance Between Two Vectors 26. Unit Vector 27. Angle Between Two Vectors 28. Orthogonal Complement

3. Properties of DeterminantLet AA and BB two n×nn×n matrices.• Determinant of an identity matrix is 11• det(A)=det(AT)det(A)=det(AT)• det(AB)=det(A)×det(B)det(AB)=det(A)×det(B)• det(AB)=det(BA)det(AB)=det(BA)• det(kA)=kndet(A)det(kA)=kndet(A)• If there is a column/row without any non-zero entry, then the determinant of that matrix is 00
4. Matrix Representation of a System of Linear EquationsA system of linear equations is a collection of two or more linear equations involving the same variables. The following is a system of linear equations with mm equations and nn unknowin variables.
a11x1+a12x2+...+a1nxn=b1
a21x1+a22x2+...+a2nxn=b2
⋮ ⋮
am1x1+am2x2+...+amnxn=bm
a11x1+a12x2+...+a1nxn=b1a21x1+a22x2+...+a2nxn=b2⋮⋮am1x1+am2x2+...+amnxn=bm The matrix representation of the the above system of linear equations is Ax=bAx=b, whereA=a
a11a12⋯a1n
a21a22⋯a2n
⋮⋮⋱⋮
am1am2⋯amn
, x=a
x1
x2
⋮
xn
, b=a
b1
b2
⋮
bm
A=[a11a12⋯a1na21a22⋯a2n⋮⋮⋱⋮am1am2⋯amn],x=[x1x2⋮xn],b=[b1b2⋮bm] • Matrix AA is reffered to as the coefficient matrix.• If b=0b=0, then the Ax=bAx=b is a homogeneous system of linear equations.
5. Solution for a System of Linear EquationsFor a system of linear equations Ax=bAx=b, xx is the unknown vector. If a vector vv satisfies Av=bAv=b, then vv is a solution for Ax=bAx=b. A system of linear equations may have1. a unique solution2. infinitely many solutions3. no solution ➔ A system of linear equations is consistent if it either has a unique solution or infinitely many solutions. ➔ A system of linear equations is inconsistent if it does not have any solution. A homogeneous system of linear equations is always consistent. Every matrix AA will have a column vector vv such that Av=0Av=0.
6. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Cramer's RuleNOTE : If the number of equations and the number of unknows are not equal then this method cannot be used. Let Ax=bAx=b be a sytem of linear equations with 33 equations and 33 variables, whereA=a
a11a12a13
a21a22a23
a31a32a33
, x=a
x
y
z
, b=a
b1
b2
b3
A=[a11a12a13a21a22a23a31a32a33],x=[xyz],b=[b1b2b3] If det(A)=0det(A)=0, then Ax=bAx=b might have infinitely many solutions or it might not have any solution. We will not be able to use the method below in that case. If det(A)≠0det(A)≠0, then Ax=bAx=b will have a unique solution. The procedure for finding that unique solution is given below. Let us define AxAx, AyAy and AzAz as follows::Ax=a
b1a12a13

, Ay=a

x=det(Ax)

det(A)
x=det(Ax)det(A)y=det(Ay)det(A)z=det(Az)det(A) A similar approach can be followed when there are nn equations and nn variables.
7. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Using Invertible Coefficient MatrixNOTE: If the number of equations and the number of unknows are not equal then this method cannot be used. Let Ax=bAx=b be a sytem of linear equations with nn equations and nn variables, where n⩾1n⩾1. If det(A)=0det(A)=0, then Ax=bAx=b might have infinitely many solutions or it might not have any solution. We will not be able to use the mthod below in that case. If det(A)≠0det(A)≠0, i.e., if AA is an invertible matrix, then Ax=bAx=b will have a unique solution. When det(A)≠0det(A)≠0, then solution for Ax=bAx=b is::
x=A-1bx=A-1b
8. Elementary Row and Column OperationsLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix.The possible row and column operations which can be performed on AA are given in the 1st column of the table below. Let BB be the matrix we get on appling one of the row/column operations. When m=nm=n, these operations might have an impact on the determinant of BB. These impacts are gievn in column 22
Elementary Row and Column OperationsImpact on det(A)det(A) if determinant exists, i.e, if m=nm=n
Switching two rows or columns.• det(A) = -det(B)det(A)=-det(B)
Adding a multiple of one row/column to another (not to the same row) row/column respectively.• det(A) = det(B)det(A)=det(B). • No change.
Multiplying a row/column by a scalar t≠0t≠0• det(A)=det(B)
tdet(A)=det(B)t
DEFINITION - LEADING ENTRYThe first non-zero entry of a row (from left to right) is the leading entry of that row.
9. Row Echelon FormLet MM be an m×nm×n matrix and let i∈N & j∈Ni∈N&j∈N, where 1<i⩽m1<i⩽m and 1<j⩽n1<j⩽n. MM is in row echelon form, if the following conditions are satisfied.1. For all the rows with atleast one non-zero entry, the leading entry must be one.2. If ithith row has a leading entry in jthjth column, then the leading entries of all the rows above this row should be to the left of the jthjth column. 3. All the rows without a non-zero entry should be below the rows with atleast one non-zero entry.
10. Reduced Row Echelon FormLet MM be an m×nm×n matrix and let j∈Nj∈N, where 1⩽j⩽n1⩽j⩽n. MM is in reduced row echelon form, if the following conditions are satisfied.1. MM should be in row echelon form2. If the jthjth column has a leading entry, then that leading entry should be the only non-zero entry in that column.
11. Solution for a System of Linear Equations :: Gaussian Elimination MethodLet Ax=bAx=b be a system of linear equations, whereA=a

A=[a11a12⋯a1na21a22⋯a2n⋮⋮⋱⋮am1am2⋯amn],x=[x1x2⋮xn],b=[b1b2⋮bm]Step-1Augment AA and bb(A|b)=a
a11a12⋯a1nb1
a21a22⋯a2nb2
⋮⋮⋱⋮⋮
am1am2⋯amnbm
(A|b)=[a11a12⋯a1nb1a21a22⋯a2nb2⋮⋮⋱⋮⋮am1am2⋯amnbm] Step-2Find the reduced row echelon form for (A|b)(A|b) using elementary row operations. Step-3Let (R|p)(R|p) be the reduced row echelon form of (A|b)(A|b) If there exists a row in (R|p)(R|p) of the form:: a
00⋯0k
[00⋯0k] where k≠0k≠0, then Ax=bAx=b does not have a solution. If Ax=bAx=b is consistent (has either unique or infinitely many solutions), then continue with the remaining steps. Step-4Let j∈Nj∈N, where 1⩽j⩽n1⩽j⩽n.• If the jthjth column of RR in (R|p)(R|p) has a leading entry of some row, then jthjth variable is a dependent variable.• If the jthjth column of RR in (R|p)(R|p) does not have a leading entry of any row, then jthjth variable is an independent variable.• If there are no independent variables, then the system has a unique solution.• If there is atleast one indepndent variable, then the system has infinitely many solutions. Go to Step-5 if there is a unique solution. Go to Step-6 if there are infinitely many solutions Step-5 (Unique solution)Convert (R|p)(R|p) into linear equations. The solution obtained from those equations is the solution for Ax=bAx=b.xample(R|p)=a

x1=0

x1+3x2+5x5=0
x1=-3x2-5x5

x3=8-7x5

-3x2-5x5,x2,8-7x5,7,x5
ax2,x5∈Ra{(-3x2-5x5,x2,8-7x5,7,x5)|x2,x5∈R}
12. Vector SpaceA non-empty set VV is a vector space with two operations • Addition (+)(+) :: V×V→VV×V→V• Scalar Multiplication (·)(·) :: R×V→VR×V→V which satisfies the following conditions for all vectors v,u,w∈Vv,u,w∈V and for all scalars a,b∈Ra,b∈R. 1. Additive Closure :: v+u∈Vv+u∈V2. Multiplicative Cloure :: a·v∈Va·v∈V3. Additive Commutativity :: u+v=v+uu+v=v+u4. Additive Associativity :: (u+v)+w=u+(v+w)(u+v)+w=u+(v+w)5. Existence of zero vector :: There should exist a zero vector v0v0 in VV such that v+v0=vv+v0=v for all v∈Vv∈V6. Additive Inverse :: For every v∈Vv∈V, there should exist a v'∈Vv'∈V such that v+v'=v0v+v'=v07. Unity :: 1·u=u1·u=u for u∈Vu∈V8. Associtivity of Multiplication :: (ab)·u=a·(b·u)(ab)·u=a·(b·u)9. Distributivity :: (a+b)·u=a·u+b·u(a+b)·u=a·u+b·u and a·(u+v)=a·u+a·va·(u+v)=a·u+a·v A vector is an element of a vector space. Remember :: Even if one of the vector space axiom fails to hold, then VV not a vector space. Zero vector is not always (0,0,...,0)(0,0,...,0). It depends of the definition of addition and scalar multiplication. Example → Linear Algebra Book (Maths-2), Example 3.2.63.2.6, Page Number 105105 The set of all the solutions for a homogeneous system of linear equations is a vector space.
13. Properties of Vector SpaceNOTE : Let VV be a vector space. Here, (⋅)(⋅) represents the scalar multiplication defined for the vector space VV. Also, 00 refers to the zero scalar and v0v0 refers to the zero vector of VV.1. Zero vector is unique.2. For each u∈Vu∈V, the additve inverse u'u' is unique.3. Cancellation Law :: If v1+v3=v2+v3v1+v3=v2+v3 for v1,v2,v3∈Vv1,v2,v3∈V, then v1=v2v1=v2.4. Cancellation Law :: If v3+v1=v3+v2v3+v1=v3+v2 for v1,v2,v3∈Vv1,v2,v3∈V, then v1=v2v1=v2.5. 0·v=v00·v=v0 for all v∈Vv∈V. Here 6. c·v0=v0c·v0=v0 for all v∈Vv∈V. Here c∈Rc∈R.7. For each c∈Rc∈R and for each u∈V u∈V, we have (−c)·u=−(c · u)=c·(−u)(−c)·u=−(c·u)=c·(−u). 8. If Ax=bAx=b and b=0b=0 (a homogeneous system), then the solution set is a Vector Space.
14. Subspace of a Vector SpaceLet VV be a vector space. Let UU be a non-empty subset of VV. UU is a subspace of VV if and only if the following conditions are true. 1. If u1,u2∈Uu1,u2∈U, then u1+u2u1+u2 should also be in UU.2. If u∈Uu∈U, then c·uc·u should also be in UU. Here, c∈Rc∈R. UU is a proper subspace of VV if UU is a subspace and also an proper subset of VV. Two trivial subspaces of a vector space VV• VV is a subspace of VV• The subset {v0}{v0} is a subspace of VV. Here, v0v0 is the zero vector of VV.
15. Linear CombinationLet VV be a vector space. Let v1,v2,...,vn∈Vv1,v2,...,vn∈V and let a1,a2,...,an∈Ra1,a2,...,an∈R.z=a1v1+a2v2+...+anvn=n∑i=1ai·viz=a1v1+a2v2+...+anvn=∑i=1nai·vizz is a linear combination of v1,v2,...,vn∈Vv1,v2,...,vn∈V.
16. Linear independence and Linear dependence.Let S={v1,v2,...,vn}S={v1,v2,...,vn} be a set of vectors in RdRd where d∈Rd∈R. Consider the following systema

[|||v1v2⋯vn|||]×[x1x2⋮xn]=[0⋮0] • If this system has a unique solution, then SS is a linearly independent set.• If this system has infinitely many solutions, the SS is a linearly dependent set. In simple words, if there exists only one way to linearly combine the vectors in set SS to get a
00⋯0
T[00⋯0]T, then SS is linearly independent. If there are more than one way to linearly combine the vectors in set SS to get a
00⋯0
T[00⋯0]T, then SS is linearly dependent. NOTE : If set SS is linearly independent, then a

17. Span SetLet S={v1,v2,...,vn}S={v1,v2,...,vn} be a set of vectors in RdRd where d∈Rd∈R. The span set of SS is defined as follows.
Span(S)={n∑i=1ai·viavi∈S and ai∈R}
Span(S)={∑i=1nai·vi|vi∈Sandai∈R}In simple words, span(S)span(S) is the set of all possible linear combinations of v1,v2,...,vnv1,v2,...,vn. NOTE : Span(S)Span(S) is a vector space.
18. Basis and Dimension of a Vector SpaceLet VV be a vector space. Let BB be a subset of VV. BB is a basis of VV if it satisfies the following conditions.• BB is a linearly independent set• Span(B)=VSpan(B)=V If BB is a basis of VV, then the dimension of VV is the cardinality of BB. In simple words, the dimension of VV is the number of elements in the basis of VV. The dimension of a vector space is unique. The basis set is not unique. A set SS is a maximal linearly independent set if SS is a linearly indepndent set any super set of SS is a linearly dependent set. A set SS is a minimal spanning set if Span(S)=VSpan(S)=V and any proper subset of SS does not span VV. The following conditions are equivalent for a set BB to be a basis of a vector space VV :1. BB is linearly independent and span(B)=Vspan(B)=V .2. BB is a maximal linearly independent set.3. BB is a minimal spanning set.
19. Finding a Basis SetLet S={v1,v2,...,vn}S={v1,v2,...,vn} be a set of vectors. We have to find the basis of Span(S)Span(S) Method 1• Let matrix AA be the following.A=a
--v1--
--v2--
⋮
--vn--
A=[--v1----v2--⋮--vn--]• Let RR be the reduced row echelon form of AA.• Let BB be the set of all the rows RR with atleast one non-zero entry. BB is a basis set for Span(S)Span(S) Method 2• Let matrix AA be the following.A=a
|| |
v1v2⋯vn
|| |
A=[|||v1v2⋯vn|||]• Let RR be the reduced row echelon form of AA.• Let B={}B={} be an empty set.• If the ithith column of RR has a leading entry of a row, then append the ithith column vector AA to the set BB.• Check this for every column in RR. In then end, set BB will be a basis for Span(S)Span(S)
20. Rank of a MatrixLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix.
The rank of AA is the number of linearly independent columns of AA
 • Column space of AA →→ Span{All the column vectors of A}Span{AllthecolumnvectorsofA}• Column rank of AA →→ dim(Column space of A)dim(ColumnspaceofA). • Row space of AA →→ Span{All the row vecors of A}Span{AlltherowvecorsofA}• Row rank of AA →→ dim(Row space of A)dim(RowspaceofA) Row Rank(A)=Column Rank(A)RowRank(A)=ColumnRank(A)
21. Nullity of a MatrixLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix. The set of all the solutions for Ax=0Ax=0 is the nullspace of AA.Nullspace(A)={x|Ax=0}Nullspace(A)={x|Ax=0} The dimension of the Nullspace(A)Nullspace(A) is the nullity of AA.
22. Rank-Nullity Theorem of MatricesLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix. Then
Rank(A)+Nullity(A)=nRank(A)+Nullity(A)=n
23. Dot ProductLet u=(u1,u2,...,un)u=(u1,u2,...,un) and let v=(v1,v2,...,vn)v=(v1,v2,...,vn), where u,v∈Rnu,v∈Rn The dot product between two uu and vv is defined asu·v=(u1×v1)+(u2×v2)+...+(un×vn)u·v=(u1×v1)+(u2×v2)+...+(un×vn) If uu and vv are column vectors, the u·vu·v can be written as uTvuTv. Let c∈Rc∈R. Some properties of dot product are as follows::• u·v=v·uu·v=v·u• cv·u=c(v·u)cv·u=c(v·u)• cu·cv=c2(u·v)cu·cv=c2(u·v)
24. Length of a VectorLet v=(a1,a2,...,an)v=(a1,a2,...,an). The length of vv is given byLength(v)=v·v=a21+a22+...+a2nLength(v)=v·v=a12+a22+...+an2
25. Euclilean Distance Between Two VectorsLet v,u∈Rnv,u∈Rn. The Euclidean distance between the two vectors is given by:||v-u||=n∑i=1(vi-ui)2||v-u||=∑i=1n(vi-ui)2
26. Unit Vectorvv is a unit vector if the length of vv is equal to 1. Let kk be a vector in RnRn. The unit vector in the direction of kk is ⟹
k
Length(k)kLength(k)
27. Angle Between Two VectorsLet u=(u1,u2,...,un)u=(u1,u2,...,un) and let v=(v1,v2,...,vn)v=(v1,v2,...,vn), where u,v∈Rnu,v∈RnLet 𝜃°𝜃° be the angle between uu and vv. Then,
𝜃=cos-1((u
Length(u))·(v
Length(v)))=cos-1((u
||u||⋅v
||v||))
𝜃=cos-1((uLength(u))·(vLength(v)))=cos-1((u||u||⋅v||v||))If the dot product between two vectors is 00, then the angle between these two vectors is 90°90°.
28. Orthogonal ComplementVV and UU are two subspaces in RnRn such that dim(V)+dim(U)=ndim(V)+dim(U)=nIf every ui∈Uui∈U is orthogonal to every vi∈Vvi∈V, then UU and VV are orthogonal complements
